scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Lysias was an orator in 4th-century BCE Athens; the speech concerns Athenian legal disabilities (atimia) for cowardice.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the approximate floruit of Lysias (-384), during the period he was active as a logographer in Athens.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The 'dike kakegorias' (action for slander) and the 500 drachmae fine are specific features of the Athenian legal system.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the approximate floruit of Lysias (-384), reflecting contemporary Athenian statutes on actionable insults.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim refers to an ancient law using archaisms like 'varlet' (oiketes) attributed to the laws of Solon.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The rule is explicitly cited as part of the Solonic code, which was established in the archonship of Solon in -594.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Lysias is quoting an 'ancient law' to interpret its meaning; these economic regulations are historically attributed to Solon.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the Solonic reforms (-594) as the speaker is quoting the legal text of that era to explain contemporary terms.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Confinement in 'the wood' (stocks) is cited as a provision of an ancient statute attributed to the laws of Solon.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the establishment of the Solonic law code (-594), which the author is quoting in court.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule refers to a specific decree passed by the Athenian assembly regarding the recovery of cavalry allowances after the fall of the Thirty Tyrants.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The recovery of the katastasis (cavalry allowance) followed the restoration of democracy in Athens in -403.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The dokimasia (scrutiny) was a standard constitutional procedure in the Athenian democracy for candidates for the Council (Boule).
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The speech 'In Defense of Mantitheus' is generally dated to approximately -392 based on internal references to recent campaigns.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The obligation of a brother to provide dowries for his sisters is a fundamental norm of Athenian family law (oikos management).
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the time of Mantitheus's trial (-392), where he cites his fulfillment of these social duties as proof of character.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The provision of 'travel money' (ephodia) for poor citizens by the wealthy falls under the category of voluntary liturgies in Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects social norms in Athens during the early 4th century BCE, specifically at the time of Mantitheus's defense (-392).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Social norms in the Athenian ekklesia discouraged very young men from speaking, despite their legal right to do so.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The speaker acknowledges this social prejudice during his scrutiny in -392.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The speech concerns the conduct of Polystratus during the oligarchic revolution in Athens in 411 BCE.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The rule describes judicial practices and social appeals during the period of political upheaval following the revolution of -411.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The liturgy system (public services) and eisphora (special levies) were central to Athenian state finance for the wealthy.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the approximate time of the trial of Polystratus (-410), following the restoration of democracy after the Four Hundred.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Eligibility for magistracies and the subsequent scrutiny of conduct were core rights and obligations of Athenian citizens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the prosecution/defense of Polystratus (-410) regarding his tenure as a magistrate.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The right to 'submit to justice' (a formal jury trial) is a hallmark of the Athenian democratic legal system.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the trial of Polystratus occurring around -410.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule describes the actions of a Registrar during the transitional regime of the Five Thousand in Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The registration process for the 'Nine Thousand' occurred during the oligarchic maneuvers of -411.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Athenian citizenship (politeia) was intrinsically linked to the right of residency and legal belonging in the city-state.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the defense of Polystratus's family (-410) against the threat of losing their civic status.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The appeal for 'pity' based on age was a recognized rhetorical and social strategy in the Athenian dikasteria (courts).
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the courtroom norms at the time of Polystratus's trial around -410.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The regulation of grain (the corn-laws) and the control of metic (resident alien) dealers were strictly managed by Athenian magistrates.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Internal evidence suggests this trial against the corn-dealers took place around -386 during a period of high grain prices.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Price gouging laws in Athens restricted the markup grain dealers could apply to essential food supplies.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the trial of the corn-dealers (-386), which addressed price manipulation during a supply crisis.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Anti-hoarding laws were enacted in Athens to prevent artificial price inflation by dealers 'buying up' supply.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The prohibition on stockpiling grain was the central legal issue in the prosecution occurring in -386.
